label: Company Profile Taxonomy
description: The set of independent, observable dimensions that characterize a company in ways that meaningfully differentiate how business problems manifest, their severity, and their likely form. INCLUDES: all structural properties that act as priors on a problem tree — industry, model, scale, phase, market, org, tech, environment, culture & brand. EXCLUDES: the problems themselves, specific strategic choices or initiatives, individual personnel, transient operational state (e.g., 'currently hiring'), and raw observational evidence (e.g., specific competitor names, recent news events) — those are inputs to filling the profile, not dimensions of the profile itself.

children.label: Industry & Vertical
children.description: The sector and sub-sector in which the company operates, determining the fundamental nature of value creation, customer expectations, supply chain shape, and competitive dynamics. INCLUDES: primary industry classification (SaaS, fintech, healthcare, education, logistics, manufacturing, retail, etc.), vertical specialization within industry, adjacent industries the company touches. EXCLUDES: business model mechanics (covered by business-model), geographic market (covered by operating-environment), company size within the industry.

children.label: Primary Sector
children.description: The top-level industry category the company belongs to, mapped to standard classifications (NAICS/GICS-like). INCLUDES: technology, healthcare, financial services, education, manufacturing, retail/e-commerce, logistics/transportation, energy, media/entertainment, professional services, real estate, agriculture. EXCLUDES: vertical specialization within sector (covered by vertical-niche), multi-industry conglomerates (use primary revenue source).

children.label: Vertical Niche
children.description: The specific sub-sector or niche that further constrains the company's problem space. INCLUDES: vertical specialization (e.g., 'edtech for K-12' within education, 'cold chain logistics' within logistics), domain-specific regulations and standards, specialized customer expectations, niche-specific competitive dynamics. EXCLUDES: the broad sector itself (covered by primary-sector), the company's specific product (that's a strategic choice, not a profile property).

children.label: Value Chain Position
children.description: Where the company sits in its industry's value chain, determining upstream/downstream dependencies and power dynamics. INCLUDES: upstream supplier/manufacturer, midstream distributor/processor, downstream retailer/end-service-provider, platform/infrastructure layer, picks-and-shovels provider. EXCLUDES: the company's internal operations structure (covered by org-structure), specific supplier relationships (transient state).

children.label: Industry Maturity
children.description: The lifecycle stage of the industry itself (not the company), which determines whether problems are about category creation, competition, or commoditization. INCLUDES: nascent/emerging (category doesn't exist yet), growth (expanding TAM, many entrants), mature (established players, incremental growth), declining (shrinking demand, consolidation). EXCLUDES: the company's own growth phase (covered by growth-phase), technology adoption curves (covered by tech-maturity).

children.label: Business Model
children.description: How the company creates, delivers, and captures value — the revenue mechanics, cost structure, and unit economics that determine which financial and operational problems are most acute. INCLUDES: revenue model (subscription, transactional, advertising, licensing), delivery model (product, service, marketplace, platform), cost structure shape (fixed-heavy vs variable-heavy), key dependencies (channel, supplier, network effects). EXCLUDES: industry classification (covered by industry-vertical), pricing strategy details (tactical, not structural), specific product features.

children.label: Revenue Model
children.description: The primary mechanism by which the company generates revenue, determining cash flow patterns, growth levers, and financial risk profile. INCLUDES: recurring subscription (SaaS, memberships), transactional/per-use, marketplace commissions/take-rate, advertising/attention monetization, licensing/IP, professional services/consulting, hardware/physical product sales, hybrid models (primary + secondary). EXCLUDES: pricing levels or tiers (tactical), revenue amount (covered by scale-resources).

children.label: Delivery Model
children.description: How value reaches the customer, determining operational complexity, scalability ceiling, and margin structure. INCLUDES: pure software product (self-serve), managed service (software + human delivery), marketplace/platform (connecting supply and demand), consulting/professional services (human-delivered), physical product (manufacturing + distribution), hybrid (e.g., SaaS + services). EXCLUDES: distribution channels (covered by market-position), technology stack (covered by tech-maturity).

children.label: Cost Structure
children.description: The shape of the company's cost base, determining breakeven dynamics, scaling economics, and vulnerability to volume changes. INCLUDES: fixed-cost-heavy (infrastructure, R&D, facilities) vs variable-cost-heavy (COGS, services delivery), capital intensity, gross margin band (high-margin software vs low-margin logistics), key cost drivers. EXCLUDES: absolute cost amounts (covered by scale-resources), specific vendor costs (transient).

children.label: Moat & Network Effects
children.description: Whether the business model has inherent defensibility through network effects, switching costs, or other structural advantages that shape competitive dynamics. INCLUDES: direct network effects (more users = more value), indirect/cross-side network effects (marketplace), data network effects, high switching costs, regulatory moats, economies of scale, brand/trust moats, no meaningful moat. EXCLUDES: competitive positioning against specific rivals (covered by market-position), IP/patents (tactical asset, not structural model property).

children.label: Key Operational Dependencies
children.description: Critical external dependencies that the business model structurally relies on, determining platform risk, supply chain vulnerability, and partner leverage. INCLUDES: platform-dependent (built on AWS/Shopify/Salesforce ecosystem, app store distribution), supply-chain-dependent (physical materials, manufacturing partners), partner-dependent (channel partners, integration partners, OEM relationships), talent-market-dependent (requires scarce specialized skills), regulation-dependent (business exists because of regulatory requirement), self-contained (minimal critical external dependencies). EXCLUDES: specific vendor names (evidence, not structure), internal operational processes (covered by org-structure), technology stack choices (covered by tech-maturity).

children.label: Scale & Resources
children.description: The quantitative size and resource base of the company, determining what is feasible, what breaks under load, and what problems are simply too expensive to have. INCLUDES: headcount bands, revenue bands, funding/capitalization, burn rate/runway, asset base. EXCLUDES: how the company is organized (covered by org-structure), what growth phase it's in (covered by growth-phase), efficiency of resource use (that's a problem, not a profile property).

children.label: Headcount Band
children.description: The number of employees, determining organizational complexity thresholds and process needs. INCLUDES: solo/micro (1-10), small (11-50), medium (51-200), large (201-1000), enterprise (1000+). Key thresholds: ~15 (need first managers), ~50 (need HR/process), ~150 (Dunbar limit — need formal structure), ~500 (need middle management layer). EXCLUDES: contractor/freelancer headcount, team composition (covered by org-structure).

children.label: Revenue Band
children.description: Annual revenue range, determining financial gravity and the scale of problems worth solving. INCLUDES: pre-revenue, early revenue (<$1M ARR), growth ($1M-$10M), scale ($10M-$100M), enterprise ($100M+). Relevant because a $500K problem is existential at $2M ARR but a rounding error at $200M. EXCLUDES: profitability (covered by financial-health), revenue model type (covered by business-model).

children.label: Capitalization & Runway
children.description: The company's financial fuel — funding raised, cash reserves, and burn rate — determining time horizon for problem-solving and risk tolerance. INCLUDES: bootstrapped/self-funded, angel/seed, Series A-C, late-stage/PE-backed, public, grant-funded (nonprofit). Also includes runway (months of cash at current burn). EXCLUDES: revenue (covered by revenue-band), investor identity or terms (transient), debt structure details.

children.label: Asset Intensity
children.description: The degree to which the company depends on physical or capital assets, determining capex needs, operational leverage, and infrastructure risk. INCLUDES: asset-light (software, consulting), asset-medium (small inventory, leased facilities), asset-heavy (manufacturing, logistics fleets, real estate, data centers). EXCLUDES: the nature of those assets (industry-specific), cost structure ratios (covered by business-model-cost-structure).

children.label: Financial Health
children.description: The company's current financial sustainability and trajectory, determining urgency of problems and risk tolerance. INCLUDES: profitability status (profitable, break-even, loss-making), cash flow direction (positive, negative, volatile), burn rate relative to revenue (sustainable, moderate burn, high burn), debt-to-equity posture (unleveraged, moderately leveraged, highly leveraged), growth trajectory signals (accelerating, steady, decelerating, contracting). EXCLUDES: revenue amount (covered by revenue-band), funding source (covered by capitalization), cost structure shape (covered by business-model-cost-structure), specific financial events (evidence, not profile dimension).

children.label: Company Age
children.description: How long the company has existed, determining institutional memory, legacy obligations, brand equity, and organizational inertia — independent of growth phase (a 50-year-old company can be scaling a new product; a 2-year-old can be in turnaround). INCLUDES: nascent (0-2 years), young (3-7 years), established (8-20 years), legacy (20+ years). EXCLUDES: growth phase (covered by growth-phase), founding story or origin (narrative, not structural).

children.label: Customer / User Base Scale
children.description: The size of the company's customer or user base, independent of revenue — determining support burden, feedback volume, network effects strength, and product decisions. INCLUDES: micro (<100 customers/users), small (100-1K), medium (1K-10K), large (10K-100K), massive (100K+). Distinguishes paying customers from free users where relevant. EXCLUDES: revenue per customer (derived from revenue band + base scale), customer segment type (covered by market-position), customer concentration (covered by market-position).

children.label: Growth Phase
children.description: The lifecycle stage the company is currently in, determining which category of problems dominates and what 'success' looks like. INCLUDES: ideation/pre-product, pre-product-market-fit, scaling, mature/optimization, decline/turnaround, exit/transition. EXCLUDES: company age in years (a proxy, not the phase itself), industry maturity (covered by industry-vertical), funding stage (correlated but independent — covered by scale-resources).

children.label: Pre-Product-Market-Fit
children.description: The company is still searching for a repeatable, scalable business. Problems center on discovery, validation, and survival. INCLUDES: ideation/concept stage, MVP/prototype, early customer discovery, pivot-ready, seeking first paying customers. EXCLUDES: companies with proven PMF that are simply small (those are in scaling or optimization).

children.label: Scaling
children.description: PMF achieved, now growing aggressively. Problems center on execution speed, hiring, infrastructure, and maintaining quality under growth. INCLUDES: rapid customer acquisition, team scaling, process formalization, market expansion, channel development. EXCLUDES: companies growing slowly by choice (those are in optimization), companies scaling revenue but not org (may be optimization with good unit economics).

children.label: Mature / Optimization
children.description: Growth has plateaued to market rate. Problems center on efficiency, retention, incremental innovation, and competitive defense. INCLUDES: market leader defending position, profitable company optimizing margins, company in steady-state seeking next growth vector, established SMB maintaining operations. EXCLUDES: companies actively declining (covered by turnaround), companies with new rapid-growth products (may be scaling in a new segment).

children.label: Decline / Turnaround
children.description: The company is contracting or facing existential strategic challenges. Problems center on survival, restructuring, pivoting, or managed wind-down. INCLUDES: revenue declining, market share eroding, major strategic pivot required, post-crisis recovery, restructuring/reorganization. EXCLUDES: seasonal or cyclical dips (normal business), voluntary downsizing for efficiency (that's optimization).

children.label: Exit / Transition
children.description: The company is preparing for or executing a major ownership transition. Problems center on transaction readiness, integration, and stakeholder management. INCLUDES: IPO preparation, acquisition target (being acquired), acquiring other companies (integration), succession planning (founder exit), wind-down/liquidation. EXCLUDES: minor leadership changes (covered by org-structure), fundraising (that's capitalization, not exit).

children.label: Market Position
children.description: Who the company serves, how it competes, and its relative standing — determining the nature of competitive pressure, sales complexity, and customer relationship dynamics. INCLUDES: target customer segments (enterprise, SMB, consumer, government), competitive positioning (leader, challenger, niche, commodity), go-to-market motion (sales-led, product-led, channel/partner), geographic market scope. EXCLUDES: industry classification (covered by industry-vertical), revenue model (covered by business-model), brand perception (subjective, not structural).

children.label: Target Customer Segment
children.description: The primary buyer profile, determining sales cycle, contract size, support expectations, and decision-making complexity. INCLUDES: enterprise (large organizations, complex procurement), mid-market/SMB (small-medium businesses), consumer/prosumer (individuals), government/public sector, nonprofit/NGO, developer/technical buyer. EXCLUDES: industry of the customer (that's industry-vertical from the customer's perspective), specific customer names.

children.label: Competitive Stance
children.description: The company's position relative to competitors in its primary market, determining strategic pressure and available moves. INCLUDES: category creator/first-mover, market leader (dominant share), strong challenger (growing share), niche specialist (defensible segment), fast follower (competing on execution), commodity player (competing on price). EXCLUDES: specific competitor names, market share percentages (quantitative detail beyond profiling).

children.label: Go-to-Market Motion
children.description: The primary mechanism for acquiring and expanding customers, determining sales team structure, marketing spend, and growth bottlenecks. INCLUDES: product-led growth (self-serve, freemium), sales-led (outbound, enterprise sales team), marketing-led (inbound, content, demand gen), channel/partner-led (resellers, integrators, OEM), community-led (open source, developer evangelism). EXCLUDES: specific marketing tactics (transient), pricing strategy (covered implicitly by business-model).

children.label: Customer Concentration
children.description: The distribution of revenue across the customer base, determining revenue risk and negotiating dynamics. INCLUDES: highly concentrated (top 5 customers = >50% revenue), moderately concentrated (top 20 = >50%), diversified (no customer >5%), single-customer dependent (government contract, anchor client). EXCLUDES: customer satisfaction or retention rates (those are outcomes, not profile properties), specific customer identities.

children.label: Pricing Position
children.description: Where the company sits on the price-value spectrum in its market, determining margin expectations, customer expectations, and competitive vulnerability. INCLUDES: premium/luxury (highest price, differentiated value), mid-market (competitive pricing, balanced value), value/low-cost (price-driven, volume-dependent), freemium (free tier + paid conversion), usage-based/transparent (pay-for-what-you-use). EXCLUDES: specific price points (too granular), revenue model mechanics (covered by business-model-revenue-model), competitive stance (covered by competitive-stance — a niche specialist can be premium or low-cost).

children.label: Organizational Structure
children.description: How the company is organized, led, and culturally oriented — determining execution capacity, internal friction points, and change readiness. INCLUDES: leadership type (founder-led, professional management, board-driven), organizational shape (flat, hierarchical, matrix), team composition (engineering-heavy, sales-heavy, balanced), culture orientation (move-fast, process-driven, consensus). EXCLUDES: headcount (covered by scale-resources), specific individuals, employee satisfaction (outcome, not structure).

children.label: Leadership Model
children.description: Who drives decisions and how, determining speed of change, strategic coherence, and succession risk. INCLUDES: solo founder, co-founder team, founder + professional CEO, professional management (no founders), board-driven/PE-controlled, family-owned/dynasty, cooperative/distributed governance. EXCLUDES: leadership quality or effectiveness (that's an outcome), specific people, board composition details.

children.label: Organizational Shape
children.description: The structural topology of teams and reporting lines, determining communication overhead, autonomy, and coordination costs. INCLUDES: flat/minimal hierarchy, functional silos (engineering, sales, ops), divisional (by product, geography, customer), matrix (dual reporting), squad/tribe model, hub-and-spoke (central team + satellite). EXCLUDES: headcount within teams (covered by scale-resources), remote/co-located (covered by workforce-model).

children.label: Workforce Model
children.description: How and where the workforce is deployed, determining collaboration patterns, talent access, and operational constraints. INCLUDES: fully co-located (single office), multi-office, remote-first, hybrid, heavily outsourced/contracted, franchise/distributed-operator model. EXCLUDES: headcount numbers (covered by scale-resources), team org chart (covered by org-shape), office locations (covered by operating-environment).

children.label: Talent Profile
children.description: The dominant skill composition of the workforce, determining what the company can execute on and where capability gaps create problems. INCLUDES: engineering/product-heavy, sales/marketing-heavy, operations-heavy, balanced, domain-expert-heavy (e.g., PhD researchers, licensed professionals), generalist/early-stage (everyone does everything). EXCLUDES: individual skill levels, hiring pipeline details (transient), compensation structure.

children.label: Technical Maturity
children.description: The sophistication, age, and capability of the company's technology infrastructure and practices, determining tech debt burden, automation potential, and ability to execute technical initiatives. INCLUDES: stack age and modernity, engineering practices (CI/CD, testing, DevOps), data infrastructure maturity, automation level, security posture, technical debt load. EXCLUDES: specific technologies used (too granular for profiling), team skill level (covered by org-structure), whether tech is the product (covered by industry-vertical).

children.label: Stack Generation
children.description: The age and architectural paradigm of the core technology stack, determining modernization burden and integration flexibility. INCLUDES: legacy/mainframe (pre-cloud, monolithic), transitional (cloud-migrating, partially modernized), cloud-native (microservices, containerized), cutting-edge (AI-native, serverless-first), no-code/low-code (built on platforms, not custom code), minimal tech (non-tech company with basic tools). EXCLUDES: specific language/framework choices, frontend vs backend distinction (too granular).

children.label: Engineering Practices
children.description: The maturity of development and delivery practices, determining release velocity, reliability, and ability to change safely. INCLUDES: ad-hoc/manual (no CI, manual deploys), basic (source control, some testing), intermediate (CI/CD, code review, staging environments), advanced (automated testing, feature flags, observability, SRE), elite (continuous deployment, chaos engineering, platform engineering). EXCLUDES: team size (covered by scale-resources), specific tools (too granular).

children.label: Data Infrastructure
children.description: The sophistication of the company's data collection, storage, and analytics capabilities, determining ability to make data-driven decisions and leverage AI/ML. INCLUDES: no structured data (spreadsheets, ad-hoc), basic analytics (BI dashboards, SQL), data warehouse (structured ETL, historical analysis), modern data stack (real-time, streaming, ML-ready), AI/ML in production (models deployed, feedback loops). EXCLUDES: what data is collected (domain-specific), data privacy compliance (covered by operating-environment).

children.label: Security Posture
children.description: The maturity of the company's security practices and infrastructure, determining vulnerability to threats and compliance readiness. INCLUDES: minimal (no formal security, reactive only), basic (firewalls, antivirus, password policies), intermediate (vulnerability scanning, incident response plan, access controls), advanced (SOC, penetration testing, zero-trust architecture, compliance certifications like SOC2/ISO27001), security-first (security as product feature, bug bounties, security engineering team). EXCLUDES: specific threats faced (outcome of profile, not the profile itself), regulatory requirements (covered by operating-environment).

children.label: Operating Environment
children.description: The external constraints, jurisdictions, and conditions under which the company operates, determining compliance burden, market access, geopolitical risk, and environmental pressures that are outside the company's control. INCLUDES: regulatory regime, geographic scope, geopolitical exposure, macroeconomic sensitivity, stakeholder/governance obligations (B-corp, nonprofit, public company). EXCLUDES: industry type (covered by industry-vertical), company's own structure (covered by org-structure), market competition (covered by market-position).

children.label: Regulatory Burden
children.description: The intensity and complexity of regulatory compliance the company must navigate, determining legal costs, operational constraints, and barrier to entry/change. INCLUDES: minimal regulation (most SaaS), moderate (data privacy — GDPR, CCPA), heavy (financial services — SEC, banking regs), very heavy (healthcare — HIPAA, FDA; defense — ITAR), industry-specific licensing requirements. EXCLUDES: the specific regulations (too granular), compliance program maturity (that's internal — covered by security-posture or org-structure).

children.label: Geographic Scope
children.description: The geographic footprint of the company's operations and markets, determining complexity of multi-jurisdiction management. INCLUDES: single-city/local, single-country/national, regional (e.g., EU, APAC), global/multi-continent. Also: whether operations (not just sales) span geographies. EXCLUDES: specific country names (too granular for profiling), workforce location (covered by org-structure-workforce-model).

children.label: Macroeconomic Sensitivity
children.description: How exposed the company is to macroeconomic cycles, interest rates, and external economic shocks, determining planning horizon and risk profile. INCLUDES: recession-resistant (essential services, government), cyclical (construction, luxury, advertising), interest-rate-sensitive (real estate, fintech), commodity-dependent (energy, agriculture), forex-exposed (multi-currency operations). EXCLUDES: the company's financial health (covered by scale-resources), specific economic forecasts.

children.label: Governance Obligations
children.description: Special obligations arising from the company's legal structure, ownership, or mission that constrain decision-making beyond normal commercial logic. INCLUDES: publicly traded (SEC reporting, quarterly pressure), B-corp/social enterprise (mission constraints), nonprofit (donor accountability, tax-exempt rules), government-owned/state-backed, VC-backed with board governance, cooperative/member-owned, family trust. EXCLUDES: internal corporate governance practices (covered by org-structure), regulatory compliance (covered by regulatory-burden).

children.label: Culture & Brand Signals
children.description: The company's cultural orientation, public identity, and innovation posture — determining change readiness, talent attraction, internal friction patterns, and how problems are framed and prioritized internally. INCLUDES: innovation posture, brand positioning and tone, public values/mission, employer brand signals, risk appetite. EXCLUDES: specific marketing campaigns (tactical), employee satisfaction scores (outcome, not structure), leadership personality (covered by org-structure-leadership), specific cultural practices (e.g., 'we do hackathons' — evidence, not dimension).

children.label: Innovation Posture
children.description: How the company relates to change, experimentation, and new approaches — determining adoption speed, risk tolerance in product/tech decisions, and internal resistance to transformation. INCLUDES: bleeding-edge (first-mover, high experimentation tolerance, 'move fast and break things'), fast-follower (adopts proven innovations quickly, moderate risk), pragmatic (adopts when clear ROI, evidence-driven), conservative (stability-first, change-averse, proven-only), regulated-cautious (would innovate but constrained by compliance burden). EXCLUDES: specific innovation projects (evidence), R&D budget (covered by cost-structure), technology stack choices (covered by tech-maturity — innovation posture is cultural, not technical).

children.label: Brand Positioning & Tone
children.description: The company's public identity and how it presents itself to market, determining customer expectations, talent attraction profile, and partnership dynamics. INCLUDES: thought-leader/visionary (shaping the category narrative), trust/reliability-first (emphasizing stability, enterprise-safe), disruptor/challenger (positioning against incumbents), community/mission-driven (purpose over profit in messaging), technical/craft-oriented (engineering excellence as brand), invisible/B2B-infrastructure (brand-minimal, product speaks). EXCLUDES: specific marketing copy (evidence), visual identity (too granular), brand awareness level (outcome of effort, not posture).

children.label: Public Values & Mission
children.description: The degree to which the company's stated values and mission constrain or guide its decisions, determining stakeholder expectations and strategic flexibility. INCLUDES: mission-driven (mission constrains commercial decisions — e.g., Patagonia, nonprofits), values-prominent (publicly stated values that influence hiring/partnerships but don't override commercial logic), commercially pragmatic (values exist but are secondary to growth/profit), values-silent (no meaningful public values stance). EXCLUDES: governance obligations (covered by operating-environment — this is about cultural identity, not legal structure), specific ESG metrics (too granular).

children.label: Employer Brand & Talent Signal
children.description: How the company is perceived as an employer, determining talent pipeline quality, retention dynamics, and the type of people who self-select in. INCLUDES: top-tier employer (attracts top talent, strong reputation — e.g., FAANG-tier), strong-in-niche (well-known in specific domain — e.g., Jane Street in quant, Stripe in payments), growing/scrappy (attracts risk-tolerant builders), stable/reliable (attracts people seeking predictability), struggling/unknown (difficulty attracting talent, high turnover signals). EXCLUDES: specific Glassdoor scores (evidence), compensation levels (covered by org-structure implicitly), specific hiring practices (tactical).
